meta.scene_summary: We are busy building a silicon utopia on top of a pile of unpaid invoices and disgruntled interns.

cooking.headline: Jim Keller wants to make chip fabs as common as coffee shops
cooking.summary: Building a custom silicon factory is usually a multi-billion dollar bet that takes years, but Jim Keller is trying to turn it into a modular IKEA project. It's the hardware equivalent of moving from a monolith to microservices, assuming the whole thing doesn't explode in the process.
cooking.plain_english: The semiconductor startup Fab2 is developing a process to mass-produce smaller, modular computer chip manufacturing facilities in Texas.

cooking.headline: AMD wants $4,000 for an AI dev kit that costs more than my entire home office setup.
cooking.summary: AMD is shipping a high-end AI development kit for the price of a used sedan, assuming you have a spare four grand lying around. It's the perfect hardware to build your next LLM, provided you don't mind eating ramen for the next six months.
cooking.plain_english: AMD has released a specialized computer hardware package designed for artificial intelligence development with a price tag of $4,000.

cooking.headline: Dartmouth students are getting tutored by AI, which is basically cheating with a permission slip.
cooking.summary: A new study shows AI tutors are actually boosting student performance significantly, proving that learning is easier when your teacher doesn't have office hours or a bad attitude. It turns out the best way to ace a class is just having a patient robot explain the syllabus until you stop failing.
cooking.plain_english: Research at Dartmouth College found that using an AI-based tutoring system significantly improved student learning outcomes in a university course.

cooking.headline: Smart home AI: Because your toaster needed a security threat model
cooking.summary: Researchers just mapped out how AI-powered home devices could turn your house into a digital hostage situation. It turns out that giving your smart fridge an LLM is a great way to ensure your kitchen appliances can be socially engineered by a teenager.
cooking.plain_english: A new research paper outlines the various security risks and social vulnerabilities introduced by integrating artificial intelligence into common household smart devices.

cooking.headline: Turns out AI agents are just as annoyed by your spaghetti code as a human intern
cooking.summary: Researchers found that AI agents actually perform better when the code they're working on is clean and well-structured. It seems even the robots can't escape the misery of trying to debug a 2,000-line function written in a fever dream.
cooking.plain_english: A study shows that artificial intelligence models are more effective at completing programming tasks when the existing code is organized and readable.

cooking.headline: Go's 'zero-copy' is the developer equivalent of trying to move house without packing a single box
cooking.summary: The dream of zero-copy is to move data around without actually touching it, but the reality involves fighting the kernel until it lets you have your way. It turns out that achieving true efficiency is just a complicated game of convincing the OS to do your heavy lifting for you.
cooking.plain_english: This article explains how Go programs can transfer data more efficiently by avoiding unnecessary memory copying using system calls like sendfile and splice.

cooked.headline: Microsoft 365 prices are up 42% because AI is expensive to maintain
cooked.summary: Microsoft is hiking prices by 42% to cover the cost of running AI, which is a bold way to tell customers they are now subsidizing your GPU addiction. It's like your landlord raising the rent because they bought a smart fridge that keeps hallucinating about the temperature.
cooked.plain_english: Microsoft has increased the subscription costs for some of its business software packages, citing the integration of artificial intelligence features as the primary reason.

cooked.headline: Zuck realizes AI agents are just interns that don't do what they're told
cooked.summary: Mark Zuckerberg is reportedly frustrated that AI agents aren't evolving fast enough to actually be useful. It turns out that teaching a model to think for itself is a lot harder than just giving it a slightly better prompt.
cooked.plain_english: Meta's CEO has informed his staff that artificial intelligence agents have not developed as quickly as he expected.

cooked.headline: The original 'AI' is finally being put out to pasture
cooked.summary: Amazon is closing the doors on Mechanical Turk, the platform that proved AI was just humans working for pennies in a trench coat. It is a poetic end for the service that basically became the training data factory for the very bots that are now replacing it.
cooked.plain_english: Amazon is no longer allowing new users to sign up for its crowdsourcing marketplace, Mechanical Turk.

cooked.headline: Canada's AI strategy is basically 'don't let the government sign secret checks for Palantir'
cooked.summary: Canada is trying to build a national AI strategy while simultaneously dealing with the optics of quiet, massive contracts with private data giants. It's like promising to eat healthy while keeping a secret stash of midnight snacks in the kitchen drawer.
cooked.plain_english: Critics are arguing that the Canadian government should not enter into undisclosed, multi-million dollar data analytics contracts with Palantir as part of its national artificial intelligence initiative.
